content:
What programming bugs, not book clubs, taught me about actually remembering what I read

6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Kelly Sikkema on UnsplashI was three chapters into a book last month when I stopped mid-sentence and thought, wait, didn’t this character already tell us this?
I flipped back. She had. Same detail, same phrasing almost, forty pages earlier. Either the editor missed it, or I was losing my mind.
Then it hit me. This wasn’t a reading problem. This was a bug.
And I know bugs. I’ve spent 15 years writing code, debugging other people’s code, and training junior developers to stop panicking every time a red error message showed up on their screen. Somewhere along the way, without meaning to, I started reading books the exact same way I read a codebase.
Nobody in my book club talks about books this way. They talk about themes, about whether they liked the ending, about what the author was “trying to say.” I sit there nodding, and in my head I’m thinking about null pointer exceptions and unhandled edge cases.
I’ve read 500+ books. Reviewed 100+ of them. I’m a featured reviewer on NetGalley. And somewhere around book 350, I realized my day job had quietly rewired how I read…
